title: Maximum Possible MEX After Operations

content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer array nums of length N and an integer value.


In one operation, you may add or subtract value from any element of the array any number of times (including zero).
Each element can be modified independently, and values may become negative.
Your task is to determine the maximum possible MEX of the array after performing these operations.
**The MEX (Minimum EXcluded number) of an array is the smallest non-negative integer that is not present in the array.**

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindMaxMexfindMaxMexfindMaxMex

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A reference to an integer array of size NNN.
valuevaluevalue : An integer that can be added to or subtracted from any array element any number of times.
Return Value
Returns an integer representing the **maximum possible MEX** that can be achieved after performing any number of operations.
Constraints
1≤T≤101 \leq T \leq 101≤T≤10
1≤N,value≤1051 \leq N, value \leq 10^51≤N,value≤105
−109≤nums[i]≤109-10^9 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^9−109≤nums[i]≤109

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains two integers NNN and valuevaluevalue — the size of the array and the operation value.
The second line contains NNN integers — the elements of the array numsnumsnums.




Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the **maximum possible MEX** after performing the operations.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers N and value. The second line contains N space‑separated integers denoting the array nums.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer – the maximum possible MEX after applying any number of allowed operations.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Residues modulo 3 are [1,2,0,1,2]. We can produce 0 using the element with residue 0, 1 using a residue‑1 element, and 2 using a residue‑2 element. No element remains with residue 0, so 3 cannot be produced. Hence MEX = 3.
Residues modulo 2 are [0,1,0,1]. We can assign them to 0,1,2,3 respectively. After using all four elements, residue 0 is exhausted, so 4 cannot be produced. MEX = 4.
When value = 1 every integer is reachable from any element, so we can create 0…5 using the six elements. The next missing number is 6.

solution.approach: Each element can be increased or decreased by multiples of value, therefore an element can be transformed into any integer that has the same remainder modulo value as the original element. Hence the whole problem reduces to the following: we have a multiset of residues (0 … value‑1) given by the array. To create the integer x we need an unused element whose residue equals x mod value. Starting from 0 we greedily try to build consecutive numbers, consuming one element of the required residue each time. The first x for which the needed residue is unavailable becomes the MEX. This greedy process is optimal because using an element for a smaller number never harms the ability to build larger numbers – larger numbers need residues that are congruent to larger remainders modulo value, which are independent of earlier choices.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, where M is the resulting MEX (at most N + value). In practice the loop runs at most N+value times, so overall O(N + value).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(value) for the residue count array.
